region_of_origin: India (Mughal / North Indian tradition); served in Singapore

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Seekh Kebab Dum Gilafi is approximately 120 g, equivalent to two skewers, as commonly served at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Seekh Kebab Dum Gilafi — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Seekh Kebab Dum Gilafi (cr_id G00010795). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for minced chicken kebab (USDA SR Legacy #05064) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for high-protein, low-carbohydrate minced meat kebab
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Seekh Kebab Dum Gilafi. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate minced meat dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 5.24 g/100g, predominantly from capsicum and tomato coating. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (30 × 5.24 × 1.20) / 100 ≈ 1.9, rounded to 2.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Seekh Kebab Dum Gilafi due to its very high sodium content of 1080 mg/100g; if consumed, restrict to a single small skewer (≤60 g) and request preparation with reduced salt and no added MSG.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and low GI (~30) are favourable, but high total fat (26.4 g/100g) may impair insulin sensitivity over time
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seekh Kebab Dum Gilafi has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it acceptable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat content (26.4 g/100g) should prompt portion control — limit to one serving (120 g) and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than rice or bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~7.2 g/100g), and very high sodium (1080 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high saturated fat (~7.2 g/100g), high total fat (26.4 g/100g), and very high sodium (1080 mg/100g) makes Seekh Kebab Dum Gilafi a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD or dyslipidaemia should consume only occasionally in small portions and prioritise lower-fat, lower-sodium protein sources.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seekh Kebab Dum Gilafi is a North Indian Mughal-origin dish consisting of spiced minced chicken moulded onto skewers and coated with a finely chopped mixture of capsicum (bell pepper), tomato, onion, green chilli, and fresh coriander — the 'gilafi' coating that gives the dish its name. The kebabs are traditionally cooked using the 'dum' technique, a slow-steam method that seals in moisture and intensifies the aromatic spice profile of cumin, garam masala, ginger, and garlic. The result is a juicy, tender kebab with a slightly smoky exterior, a fragrant herb-and-capsicum crust, and a mildly spiced interior. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as a starter or main course at lunch and dinner. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat, and only 5.24 g carbohydrate, placing it in the low-glycemic-index category (estimated GI ~30). Sodium is very high at 1080 mg/100g, making it a high-concern food for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal when prepared with chicken. Clinically, it is acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes management in controlled portions due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile, but the high fat and sodium content require caution for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or dyslipidaemia.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010795, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest match and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source and is plausible given the minimal vegetable coating relative to the minced meat base. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match to this preparation as of 2026. 3. Saturated fat (7.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy entry for ground chicken, cooked (USDA #05064), adjusted for the fat content reported by HPB; this is an approximation and should be updated if HPB publishes fatty acid data for this item. 4. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis referenced red meat (lamb/beef) as the primary ingredient; however, the HPB description and dish name confirm minced chicken as the primary protein. The rheumatoid arthritis entry has been excluded from health_context as it was based on an incorrect ingredient assumption. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (~30, low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database framework for high-protein, low-carbohydrate minced meat dishes; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in published literature as of 2026.
